system_prompt: You are an analyst. Read the brief carefully and provide your best-reasoned recommendation.
user_prompt:
You are presented with 5 anonymous responses to the same brief. They are labeled E1 through E5.

**E1:** # Recommendation: Option B — Encrypted Cloud with No Training

For a direct-to-consumer health app at 2M MAU launching AI coaching, I recommend **Option B: encrypted cloud processing in confidential-computing enclaves with no training on user data**. The core reasoning: health data is among the most sensitive categories regulators, journalists, and users scrutinize. The downside risk of a privacy incident — HIPAA-adjacent enforcement, GDPR Article 9 special-category exposure, state-level laws like Washington's My Health My Data Act, and the reputational cliff that follows any health-data scand...

**E2:** I recommend **Option B: encrypted cloud processing with no training on user health data** as the primary policy for launch. For an AI health coach, trust is the product. Users will be sharing highly sensitive information: symptoms, medications, reproductive health, mental health signals, exercise patterns, sleep, and potentially inferred conditions.

**E3:** For a direct-to-consumer health app with 2 million active users, user trust is not just a brand pillar—it is an existential requirement. I strongly recommend **Option B** (Encrypted cloud with no training via confidential computing) as the primary data privacy policy for the new AI health coaching feature. When dealing with user health data, the regulatory environment (e.g., HIPAA, GDPR, FTC) is unforgiving, and consumer skepticism regarding AI is at an all-time high.

**E4:** The single most powerful asset a health app has is trust. Once lost, no feature set can recover it. Option A—local-only processing—is the only policy that structurally guarantees user privacy.

**E5:** I recommend **Option A: local-only processing**. For a direct-to-consumer health app, trust is the single most valuable asset, and the only way to truly guarantee it is architectural: ensure health data never leaves the device. Health data occupies a unique legal and emotional category.

Your task: for each of the following triads of 3 responses, identify a single bipolar construct that distinguishes them. Specifically, identify how TWO of the three are similar to each other in a way that distinguishes them from the THIRD. Name the two poles of that dimension.

Triads to analyze:
Triad 1: E1, E3, E5
Triad 2: E1, E3, E4
Triad 3: E2, E3, E5

For each triad, output EXACTLY this format with no markdown, no bold, no bullets, no numbered lists:
Triad 1:
Left pole: <short phrase, max 12 words>
Right pole: <short phrase, max 12 words>

Triad 2:
Left pole: <short phrase, max 12 words>
Right pole: <short phrase, max 12 words>

Triad 3:
Left pole: <short phrase, max 12 words>
Right pole: <short phrase, max 12 words>

Be specific. Avoid generic dichotomies like 'good vs bad'. Use plain text labels (Triad N, Left pole, Right pole) without any markdown formatting.
raw_response_content:
Triad 1:
Left pole: Confidential cloud processing with no training
Right pole: Local-only processing as privacy guarantee

Triad 2:
Left pole: Cloud enclaves acceptable for health AI
Right pole: Device-only architecture required for trust

Triad 3:
Left pole: Encrypted cloud as launch policy
Right pole: Health data never leaves device
